title: SACRAMENTO
prompt_preview:

raw_text:
```json
{
  "claims": [
    "If enacted, Assembly Bill 1893 will modernize and improve the efficacy of the builder’s remedy.",
    "The builder’s remedy limits the ability of local governments to restrict the development of new affordable and mixed-income housing projects.",
    "It has been over 30 years since the builder's remedy was enacted and it's remained largely unchanged since then.",
    "AB 1893 will ensure that even cities without up-to-date housing plans continue to develop affordable housing.",
    "AB 1893 will provide clearer guidelines to local governments, planners, developers, and courts.",
    "AB 1893 is expected to require all cities and counties to participate in addressing the housing crisis.",
    "The builder’s remedy provision prohibits local governments from denying certain housing projects for inconsistency with zoning or land use designation.",
    "The housing element law is critical for California because it helps cities and counties plan for their regional housing needs.",
    "The failure to adopt a compliant housing element prejudices residents and neighboring jurisdictions.",
    "Many noncompliant jurisdictions have not been processing builder’s remedy applications in a timely fashion.",
    "AB 1893 aims to reduce disputes and litigation that impede housing development.",
    "AB 1893 will provide clear objective standards for residential development, including upzoning provisions.",
    "Upzoning can allow for doubling or tripling the density of development allowed under current zoning laws.",
    "AB 1893 will incentivize housing construction by making development projects more feasible.",
    "Exempting sites with 10 units and fewer from affordability requirements will enable more financially feasible housing projects.",
    "Reducing the affordability requirement for mixed-income projects from 20% to 10% will enable more development projects.",
    "'Missing middle' projects are expected to serve the needs of more diverse households and are generally more affordable than single-family homes."
  ]
}
```
